Jimmy Driftwood's 1936 classic "The battle of New Orleans" is desperately in need of a rewrite since "Gulf of Mexico" has been replaced by "Gulf of America," and today's puzzle has provided me with the rhyming word I needed: erica/Erica. I'm open to suggestions as to how to work it in without making it sound too contrived. (Many years ago, I published a challenge to readers to rewrite the Gettysburg Address in limerick form and got several ingenious responses.)

So very good puzzle overall, just a couple very Naticky areas at the end.
Clever. Entertaining. Meh.

I am very glad that the constructor put the O in the line over where the H's are, rather than H-O-H.  

Most of you probably know this, but the bond angle of the H-O-H bond is about 105 degrees.  Not quite the right angle suggested here, but close.

That makes all the difference.  If the angle were 180 degree, as H-O-H suggest, water would have no dipole moment.  It acts like it has a positive and a negative end.

No dipole moment would have enormous effects.  Water is incredibly unusual in that:

1) it has a *much* higher boiling point that it "should" compared 
 o H-S-H and H-Se-H, so it is liquid at relatively high temperatures, 

2) water can settle into a liquid packed just a bit more tightly than in solid ice.  A given volume of ice weighs less than the same volume of liquid water.  This means water freezes from the top, and the underlying layer remains at 0 C.  Aquatic life can survive winter, if it doesn't get too cold.

3) water needs a *lot* of energy to change from solid to liquid, and even more to change from liquid to gas.  Evaporating water gets cooler, and helps to keep us cool in hot climates. It's also why steam burns are so serious--not only is the water vapor hot, but it releases a huge amount of energy when it condenses into a liquid.

Water has so many anomalous properties, if it weren't so common (and if life were even possible without so much of it) we'd consider it a laboratory oddity.

Musician here.  Regarding 40A, “ARCO” in the score means that the note is to be played by bowing (as opposed to plucking, “pizzicato”).  But it doesn’t indicate a direction.  That’s worked out by the first chair violinist (same for viola, cello, string bass) who then has the others mark their parts so that the bows will move in unison during the performance. 

NB I play piano and low brass 🤣

"Gal Friday" refers back to Robinson Crusoe by Daniel Defoe. Crusoe meets a native on the island, names him Friday, and Friday does a lot of varying tasks for him. "Friday" or "Man Friday" became a term for a versatile and capable servant or worker. "Gal Friday" followed as the female version.

Simeon, I, for one, would have loved having the grid circle-less.  I respect those who feel differently, but here are my reasons:

•	The puzzle would have had Thursday-worthy riddles to crack that it doesn’t have now.  My brain would have savored figuring out what was going on, including finding the H-O-H clusters and figuring out that they were rebuses representing “water”. These revelations would have been aha-producing.  
•	None of this riddle-cracking happened with today’s grid configuration.  Seeing the first H-O-H cluster gave away the rest, including the water connection.
•	After that, the reveal WATER just, IMO, fell flat, didn’t feel needed or revelatory.  Without the circles, it would have been clued something like [Caret-shaped compound depicted four times in this puzzle], and had I not figured out what was going on, it would have set me on an aha-filled journey, ending in a cloud of wow.  Without the circles, the reveal is perfect.

Just one solver’s opinion, Simeon.  I was happy to learn you turned this puzzle in without the circles. That version made this puzzle consistent with the sterling quality of all your others.

Also, fun puzzle for a classicist, with the inclusion of real people--DRACO, CATO--and real phrases/things--KITHARA, PER DIEM, FIAT, and I suppose PSI--but also some "accidentals"--ISTO would. in Latin, be the ablative masculine singular of "iste," meaning "that man," but "that" said with a sneer--"that man over there who smells, and probably hangs out with Carthaginians," as Cato might say.
Although both ARCO and MANO derive from Latin, neither form actually exists in Classical Latin, as both "arcus" and "manus" are 4th declension nouns, which became 2nd declension ones in post-Classical times, when they stopped using the 4th, before moving on to Italian and Spanish.

On GAL Friday: this goes back to Daniel Defoe's book _Robinson Crusoe_. Back then it was common for upper class men to have a manservant, ofthen just called "a man". Robinson Crusoe was shipwrecked on an island, and comes across another inhabitant, who becomes his servant. (I'm not sure just why the other dude agreed to do this as I haven't read the book.) He names the servant Friday because that's the day they met. So this servant was "his man, Friday". 

So then in 1940 we have a screwball comedy in which girl reporter Rosalind Russell intends to make herself invaluable to a newspaper editor. Effectively she wants to become his "do-everything". So then she's a "man Friday" — but she can't be a man, she's a girl. So "girl Friday". And then we go from girl to gal.
What fun! Thank you.

@Steve L The definition of rebus is "a representation of words or syllables by pictures of objects or by symbols whose names resemble the intended words or syllables in sound". The molecule is a symbol that represents water. It's a rebus.

@Eric

That's not how "rebus" is used in crossword terminology.  In crosswords, "rebus" specifically means "numerals or symbols or multiple letters that appear in one cell, as opposed to a single letter, which is the norm."

Using "rebus" to mean anything else in this context would serve only to confuse anyone whose skill level is such that they require an explanation of the theme.  It is counterproductive, especially for a columnist entrusted to guide new solvers, to introduce the word in this context.

Even if all the above were not true, what happens in this puzzle does not satisfy the definition you cite for the older, non-crossword usage of "rebus."  As you state, that kind of rebus is a puzzle with pictures, symbols, and a few letters that approximate the sound of the solution.  This was most famously seen in the classic game show, Concentration. But in today's puzzle, H-O-H does not have any relation to the sound of the word "water," so your argument goes out the window.  

Not every clever trick involving outside-the-box thinking is a rebus.  Of either type.

@Steve L It's a visual representation of water. It's a rebus. It may be confusing, but it is accurate. 

If the lines and circles were left out, then I'd agree with you.

@Eric Reread the definition you cited. This puzzle doesn't qualify. A visual representation of something doesn't make it automatically a rebus. Even if it fit that definition, it is misleading to new audiences because that is not how it is used in a crossword context. Even if it were technically accurate, this kind of trick should not be discussed as a rebus, as that means something different in crossword terminology. It's deeply disturbing to me that you're getting so many recommendations, but I'm aware that there have been huge numbers of new signups, and now all these people are confused. Regulars who have been solving for decades and know the terminology would never call this a rebus. If you are new here, you should learn from those of us who are longtime solvers. If you're not new, you're just being obtuse. Here is a list of every rebus puzzle in the NYT crossword's history from xwordinfo.com, the ultimate source of all NYT crossword information. It is updated immediately when every puzzle comes out. Note that today's puzzle is not listed, nor is any other similar puzzle. https://www.xwordinfo.com/Rebus If you come back at me that it's a rebus after what I've just said, I'm not engaging anymore. It's pointless to argue with entrenched, repeated misinformation that won't budge.

@Steve L

As much as I hate to jump into someone else’s argument, I must concur with Eric.  Picture rebuses are my favorite kind of puzzle, and I have solved more of them than I can count. They contain images that are clues to intended words. Some are very literal, some have to be intuited depending on context. But when put together and sounded out, they create a word or phrase. The H2O molecule, as pictured here, would fit well into any picture rebus because it represents water. In a different rebus, it could also represent “molecule”— as I said, context is important. That’s part of the puzzle.

I concede that this is not the type of rebus that crossword creators usually employ, and that may be confusing to some solvers. But creating a puzzle challenge is what Thursday is all about. And if that causes people to open their minds to new definitions and different types of puzzles, I am hard pressed to see the harm in that.

If you don’t like thinking of this as a rebus, then by all means call it something else. But it IS, by definition, a rebus.

No sound. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/rebus The example given in the link shows what is meant by the traditional meaning of "rebus." There has to be a hint to the **sound** of what is being represented. So a tomato soup can can stand for the word "can," and a water well can stand for "well" meaning "in a good manner." Spelling doesn't factor in here, just sound, so a picture of an eye can stand for "I." H-O-H doesn't sound like "water," even if it represents it. So it's not a traditional rebus.

@The X-Phile

Every endeavor has its specific usage.  Math has lines and line segments, but in common parlance, you stand on a line at the store, not a line segment.  Crossword lingo can contain "rebus" with a different meaning than the original one, as long as everyone understands what is meant.

Xwordinfo.com did not list this puzzle on its list of rebus puzzles because it doesn't contain a rebus in the crossword sense.  (There is only one meaning in the crossword sense.)  I would argue that it doesn't even contain a traditional rebus, because in a traditional rebus, the representation must evoke the sound of what it's representing, and H-O-H is not pronounced "water." (See my reply just above to Katie).

Maybe this example makes it clearer.  In football, there are punters and there are kickers.  Kickers are the guys who kick from a tee for kickoffs, and attempt points after and field goals, in which case the ball is held on the ground by a teammate.  Punters punt the ball, which is to say, they release the ball and kick it while it's still in the air.  It's undoubtedly a form of kicking, and technically a punter is a kicker in the non-football sense of the word (i.e. simply one who kicks), but it would be confusing to call him a kicker when a kicker has a different role and technique.  It is pointless to insist that a punter is a kicker, even though in a non-specific sense he is, because it leads to unnecessary confusion.

Hope that clarifies!

commentBody:
@The X-Phile
Yes but the NYT version of rebus seems to exist here and nowhere else.  From a linguistics point of view, that reduces it to jargon -- still crossword-kosher but not really capable of being part of the assumed language. (I always cherish the great translator Benjamin Harkavy's distinction between a language and a jargon: "A language is a jargon with a navy.") Here /and seemingly only here/ "rebus" means the process of squishing multiple letters into a single space. So it's fine for the NYT-ists to have this individual meaning but it scuttles the purpose of language--to communicate.
